old_claim.note: Monasticism and Scriptoriums provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: Early medieval illuminated manuscripts were copied and illuminated in scriptoria, with monasteries serving as major production settings.

source_shape.source_locator: Getty describes the scriptorium as the medieval setting where books were copied and illuminated by hand, tying manuscript illumination to monastic and scribal production spaces.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source connects medieval scriptoria to hand copying and illumination of books.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: The edge is enabling because monasteries and scriptoria explain a dominant production setting without claiming every illuminated manuscript was made in a monastery.

invariant_preserved_or_changed: Preserved: illuminated_manuscripts_scribal_art keeps monasticism_and_scriptoria as an enabling production-setting edge.
would_reject_if: The node is narrowed to late medieval secular commercial workshops only.
A stronger source shows the seventh-century anchor should be represented by a specific Insular monastery node.
monasticism_and_scriptoria is split into separate monasticism and scriptorium nodes that require a more specific production edge.
short_reason: Scriptoria are the sourced early production setting.
